question_text: Which sugar is called invert sugar? Why is it called so?
question_html: Which sugar is called invert sugar? Why is it called so?
solution_text: Sucrose is called invert sugar. The sugar obtained from sugar beet is a colourless, crystalline and sweet substance. It is very soluble in water and its aqueous solution is dextrorotatory having {tex}[\alpha]_{\mathrm{D}}=+66.5^{\circ}{/tex} . On hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzyme invertase, cane sugar gives an equimolar mixture of D - (+)-glucose and D-(-)-fructose. So, sucrose is dextrorotatory but after hydrolysis gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. D-(-)-fructose has a greater specific rotation than D-(+)-glucose. Therefore, the resultant solution upon hydrolysis is laevorotatory in nature. {tex}{/tex} Since the laevorotation of fructose (–92.4°) is more than dextrorotation of glucose (+ 52.5°). Thus, hydrolysis brings change in the sign of rotation from dextro before hydrolysis to laevo after hydrolysis, the reaction is called inversion reaction and the mixture (glucose and fructose) is called invert sugar.
solution_html:
Sucrose is called invert sugar. The sugar obtained from sugar beet is a colourless, crystalline and sweet substance. It is very soluble in water and its aqueous solution is dextrorotatory having {tex}[\alpha]_{\mathrm{D}}=+66.5^{\circ}{/tex}. On hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzyme invertase, cane
sugar gives an equimolar mixture of D - (+)-glucose and D-(-)-fructose.

So, sucrose is dextrorotatory but after hydrolysis gives dextrorotatory glucose and laevorotatory fructose. D-(-)-fructose has a greater specific rotation than D-(+)-glucose. Therefore, the resultant solution upon hydrolysis is laevorotatory in nature. {tex}{/tex}Since the laevorotation of fructose (–92.4°) is more than dextrorotation of glucose (+ 52.5°). Thus, hydrolysis brings change in the sign of rotation from dextro before hydrolysis to laevo after hydrolysis, the reaction is called inversion reaction and the mixture (glucose and fructose) is called invert sugar.

question_html: Sucrose is dextrorotatory but the mixture obtained after hydrolysis is laevorotatory. Explain.
solution_text: Sucrose is dextrorotatory having {tex}[\alpha]_{\mathrm{D}}=+66.5^{\circ} {/tex} . On hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzymes, it gives equimolar dextrorotatary glucose and laevorotatory-fructose {tex}\underset{Sucrose}{{\mathrm C}_{12}{\mathrm H}_{22}{\mathrm O}_{11}}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\overset{\mathrm{HCl}}\longrightarrow\underset{\mathrm D-(+)-\mathrm{glucose}\\{\lbrack\mathrm\alpha\rbrack}_{\mathrm D}=\;+52.4^\circ}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_{12}{\mathrm O}_6}+\underset{\mathrm D-(+)-\mathrm{Fructose}\\{\lbrack\mathrm\alpha\rbrack}_{\mathrm D}=\;-92.4^\circ}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_{12}{\mathrm O}_6}{/tex} Hence, Dextrorotation of glucose +52.5 is less than laevorotation of fructose -92.5 {tex}{/tex} therefore, the mixture is laevorotatory.
solution_html:
Sucrose is dextrorotatory having {tex}[\alpha]_{\mathrm{D}}=+66.5^{\circ} {/tex}. On hydrolysis with dilute acids or enzymes, it gives equimolar dextrorotatary glucose and laevorotatory-fructose
{tex}\underset{Sucrose}{{\mathrm C}_{12}{\mathrm H}_{22}{\mathrm O}_{11}}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\overset{\mathrm{HCl}}\longrightarrow\underset{\mathrm D-(+)-\mathrm{glucose}\\{\lbrack\mathrm\alpha\rbrack}_{\mathrm D}=\;+52.4^\circ}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_{12}{\mathrm O}_6}+\underset{\mathrm D-(+)-\mathrm{Fructose}\\{\lbrack\mathrm\alpha\rbrack}_{\mathrm D}=\;-92.4^\circ}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_{12}{\mathrm O}_6}{/tex}
Hence, Dextrorotation of glucose +52.5 is less than laevorotation of fructose -92.5{tex}{/tex} therefore, the mixture is laevorotatory.

question_text: Protein found in a biological system with a unique three-dimensional structure and biological activity is called a native protein. When a protein in its native form, is subjected to a physical change like change in temperature or a chemical change like change in pH, denaturation of protein takes place. Explain the cause.
question_html: Protein found in a biological system with a unique three-dimensional structure and biological activity is called a native protein. When a protein in its native form, is subjected to a physical change like change in temperature or a chemical change like change in pH, denaturation of protein takes place. Explain the cause.
solution_text: Due to physical (change in temperature) or chemical change (change in pH), hydrogen bonding and various other attractive forces are disturbed, globules unfold and helix gets uncoiled to form a thread-like a molecule. Therefore, the secondary and tertiary structure of protein loses all or part of their biological activity. This is called the denaturation of proteins.
solution_html:
Due to physical (change in temperature) or chemical change (change in pH), hydrogen bonding and various other attractive forces are disturbed, globules unfold and helix gets uncoiled to form a thread-like a molecule.
Therefore, the secondary and tertiary structure of protein loses all or part of their biological activity. This is called the denaturation of proteins.

question_text: The activation energy for the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of sucrose is 6.22 kJ mol –1 , while the activation energy is only 2.15 kJ mol –1 when hydrolysis is catalyzed by the enzyme sucrase. Explain.
question_html: The activation energy for the acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of sucrose is 6.22 kJ mol–1, while the activation energy is only 2.15 kJ mol–1 when hydrolysis is catalyzed by the enzyme sucrase. Explain.
solution_text: Enzymes are the biocatalysts. They reduce the magnitude of the activation energy by providing an alternative path increases the rate of reaction. In the hydrolysis of sucrose, the enzyme sucrase reduces the activation energy from 6.22 kJ mol –1 to 2.15 kJ mol –1 . As a result, enzyme-catalyzed reactions occur at a much faster rate than ordinary chemical reactions using the conventional catalysts.

question_text: Describe the terms D- and L- configuration used for amino acids with examples.
question_html: Describe the terms D- and L- configuration used for amino acids with examples.
solution_text: The sugars are divided into two families: the D-family and L-family which have definite configurations. These relative configurations are represented with respect to glyceraldehyde as the standard. The glyceraldehydes may be represented in two forms as: All the compound which can be chemically correlated to (+) isomers of glyceraldehyde are said to be D- configuration All the compound which can be chemically correlated to (-) isomers are said to be L-configuration. The D-configuration has -OH attached to the carbon adjacent to -CH 2 OH on the right while L - configuration has - OH attached to the carbon adjacent to -CH 2 OH on left. The sugars are called D - or L - depending upon whether the configuration of the molecule is related to D-glyceraldehyde or L-glyceraldehyde. It has been found that all naturally occurring sugars belong to D-series, e.g., D-glucose and D-fructose. (D-glucose and L- glucose) (D-fructose).
solution_html:
The sugars are divided into two families: the D-family and L-family which have definite configurations. These relative configurations are represented with respect to glyceraldehyde as the standard. The glyceraldehydes may be represented in two forms as:

  1. All the compound which can be chemically correlated to (+) isomers of glyceraldehyde are said to be D- configuration
  2. All the compound which can be chemically correlated to (-) isomers are said to be L-configuration.

The D-configuration has -OH attached to the carbon adjacent to -CH2OH on the right while L - configuration has - OH attached to the carbon adjacent to -CH2OH on left. The sugars are called D - or L - depending upon whether the configuration of the molecule is related to D-glyceraldehyde or L-glyceraldehyde. It has been found that all naturally occurring sugars belong to D-series, e.g., D-glucose and D-fructose.

solution_text: When an egg is boiled, the soluble globular protein, albumin present in it is converted into insoluble fibrous protein. question_text: Write the reactions of D-glucose which can’t be explained by its open-chain structure. How can the cyclic structure of glucose explain these reactions?
question_html: Write the reactions of D-glucose which can’t be explained by its open-chain structure. How can the cyclic structure of glucose explain these reactions?
solution_text: The open-chain structure of glucose explained most of its properties. However, it could not explain the following facts. Despite having an aldehydic (-CHO) group, glucose does not undergo certain characteristic Reactions of aldehydes. For example, Glucose does not react with ammonia. Glucose does not react with sodium bisulphite (NaHSO 3 ) to form an addition product hydrogen sulphite. Glucose does not give Schiff's test and 2,4 -DNP test like other aldehydes. Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine (-NH 2 OH) to form an oxime but glucose pentaacetate does not react with hydroxylamine. This shows that free -CHO groups are not present in glucose pentaacetate D(+)- Glucose exists in two stereoisomeric forms i.e., {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. The {tex}\alpha{/tex} - form of glucose(.m.p.419K) is obtained by crystallisation from conc. solution the glucose at 303K while {tex}\beta{/tex} - form (.m.p. 423K) is obtained by crystallisation from hot and saturated aq. solution at 371K. An aqueous solution of glucose shows mutarotation, i.e., its specific rotation gradually decreases from {tex}+110^{\circ}{/tex} to {tex}+52.5^{\circ}{/tex} in case of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -glucose and increases from {tex}+19.7^{\circ}{/tex} to {tex}+52.5^{\circ}{/tex} in case of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -glucose. Glucose forms isomeric methyl glucosides. When glucose is heated with methanol in the presence of dry hydrogen chloride gas, it gives two isomeric monomethyl derivatives known as methyl {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucoside (m.p. = 438 K or {tex}165^{\circ}{/tex} C) and methyl {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucoside (m.p. = 380 K or {tex}107^{\circ}{/tex} C) These results show that glucose does not have an open-chain form structure.
solution_html:
The open-chain structure of glucose explained most of its properties. However, it could not explain the following facts.

  1. Despite having an aldehydic (-CHO) group, glucose does not undergo certain characteristic Reactions of aldehydes. For example,
    1. Glucose does not react with ammonia.
    2. Glucose does not react with sodium bisulphite (NaHSO3) to form an addition product hydrogen sulphite.
    3. Glucose does not give Schiff's test and 2,4 -DNP test like other aldehydes.
  2. Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine (-NH2OH) to form an oxime but glucose pentaacetate does not react with hydroxylamine. This shows that free -CHO groups are not present in glucose pentaacetate
  3. D(+)- Glucose exists in two stereoisomeric forms i.e., {tex}\alpha{/tex} -D-glucose and {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucose. The {tex}\alpha{/tex}- form of glucose(.m.p.419K) is obtained by crystallisation from conc. solution the glucose at 303K while {tex}\beta{/tex}- form (.m.p. 423K) is obtained by crystallisation from hot and saturated aq. solution at 371K.
  4. An aqueous solution of glucose shows mutarotation, i.e., its specific rotation gradually decreases from {tex}+110^{\circ}{/tex} to {tex}+52.5^{\circ}{/tex} in case of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-glucose and increases from {tex}+19.7^{\circ}{/tex} to {tex}+52.5^{\circ}{/tex} in case of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-glucose.
  5. Glucose forms isomeric methyl glucosides. When glucose is heated with methanol in the presence of dry hydrogen chloride gas, it gives two isomeric monomethyl derivatives known as methyl {tex}\alpha{/tex}-D-glucoside (m.p. = 438 K or {tex}165^{\circ}{/tex} C) and methyl {tex}\beta{/tex} -D-glucoside (m.p. = 380 K or {tex}107^{\circ}{/tex}C)

    These results show that glucose does not have an open-chain form structure.

question_text: On the basis of which evidence D-glucose was assigned the following structure?
question_html: On the basis of which evidence D-glucose was assigned the following structure?
solution_text: Glucose is an aldohexose and is also known as dextrose. It was assigned the structure given below on the basis of the following evidence: Molecular formula: The molecular formula of glucose has been found to be C 6 H 12 O 6 Straight chain structure: When an aqueous solution of glucose is treated with sodium amalgam (Na/Hg) or sodium borohydride, it is reduced to sorbitol (or glucitol) a hexahydric alcohol. {tex}\overset{CHO}{\underset{CH_2OH}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{(CH\mathrm{OH})_4}}}}+2\lbrack\mathrm H\rbrack\xrightarrow{Na\;amalgam}\overset{CH_2OH}{\underset{CH_2OH\\Sorbitol}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{(\mathrm{CHOH})_4}}}}{/tex} Prolonged heating with hydriodic acid and red phosphorus at {tex}100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex} gives'a mixture of n-hexane and 2 -iodohexane. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\underset{Glu\cos e}{(\mathrm{CHOH})_4}\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[{red\;P,\;100^\circ C}]{HI}C\underset{n-hexane}{H_3{\left({\mathrm{CH}}_2\right)}_4}C\underset{2-Iodohexane}{\underset I{\underset\vert{H_3+{\mathrm{CH}}_3CH{\left({\mathrm{CH}}_2\right)}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_3}}}{/tex} The formation of n-hexane suggests that all six carbon atoms in glucose are arranged in a straight chain structure of glucose. Presence of five hydroxyl (-OH) groups: On acetylation with acetic anhydride, glucose gives a pentaacetate. This confirms that glucose contains five -OH groups. We know that the presence of two or more -OH groups on the same carbon atom makes the molecules unstable. Now since glucose is a stable compound, therefore, the five -OH groups must present on different carbon atoms. Presence of one primary alcoholic group: On oxidation with cone, nitric acid, both glucose and gluconic acid give the same dicarboxylic acid, saccharic acid or glucaric acid. The primary alcoholic group (CH 2 OH) is always present at the end of the carbon chain. On oxidation with nitric acid, glucose as well as gluconic acid both yield a dicarboxylic acid, saccharic acid. this indicates the presence of a primary alcoholic (-OH) group in glucose. The presence of an aldehyde (-CHO) group: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine, NH 2 OH to form glucose CHO oxime. This suggests that glucose contains a carbonyl (CHOH) 4 (>C = O) group.
solution_html:
Glucose is an aldohexose and is also known as dextrose. It was assigned the structure given below on the basis of the following evidence:

  1. Molecular formula: The molecular formula of glucose has been found to be C6H12O6
  2. Straight chain structure:
    1. When an aqueous solution of glucose is treated with sodium amalgam (Na/Hg) or sodium borohydride, it is reduced to sorbitol (or glucitol) a hexahydric alcohol.
      {tex}\overset{CHO}{\underset{CH_2OH}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{(CH\mathrm{OH})_4}}}}+2\lbrack\mathrm H\rbrack\xrightarrow{Na\;amalgam}\overset{CH_2OH}{\underset{CH_2OH\\Sorbitol}{\overset\vert{\underset\vert{(\mathrm{CHOH})_4}}}}{/tex}
    2. Prolonged heating with hydriodic acid and red phosphorus at {tex}100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex} gives'a mixture of n-hexane and 2 -iodohexane.
      {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\underset{Glu\cos e}{(\mathrm{CHOH})_4}\mathrm{CHO}\;\xrightarrow[{red\;P,\;100^\circ C}]{HI}C\underset{n-hexane}{H_3{\left({\mathrm{CH}}_2\right)}_4}C\underset{2-Iodohexane}{\underset I{\underset\vert{H_3+{\mathrm{CH}}_3CH{\left({\mathrm{CH}}_2\right)}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_3}}}{/tex}
      The formation of n-hexane suggests that all six carbon atoms in glucose are arranged in a straight chain structure of glucose.
  3. Presence of five hydroxyl (-OH) groups: On acetylation with acetic anhydride, glucose gives a pentaacetate. This confirms that glucose contains five -OH groups. We know that the presence of two or more -OH groups on the same carbon atom makes the molecules unstable. Now since glucose is a stable compound, therefore, the five -OH groups must present on different carbon atoms. Presence of one primary alcoholic group: On oxidation with cone, nitric acid, both glucose and gluconic acid give the same dicarboxylic acid, saccharic acid or glucaric acid. The primary alcoholic group (CH2OH) is always present at the end of the carbon chain.
  4. On oxidation with nitric acid, glucose as well as gluconic acid both yield a dicarboxylic acid, saccharic acid. this indicates the presence of a primary alcoholic (-OH) group in glucose. 
  5. The presence of an aldehyde (-CHO) group: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine, NH2OH to form glucose CHO oxime. This suggests that glucose contains a carbonyl (CHOH)4(>C = O) group.

question_text: Carbohydrates are essential for life in both plants and animals. Name the carbohydrates that are used as storage molecules in plants and animals, also name the carbohydrate which is present in wood or in the fibre of cotton cloth.
question_html: Carbohydrates are essential for life in both plants and animals. Name the carbohydrates that are used as storage molecules in plants and animals, also name the carbohydrate which is present in wood or in the fibre of cotton cloth.
solution_text: Carbohydrate is essential for life and also used as storage molecules in plants and animals are as follows: The plant contains mainly starch, cellulose, sucrose, etc. Animal contains glycogen in their body. So, glycogen is also known as animal starch. Glycogen is present in the liver, muscles, and brain when the body needs glucose, the enzyme breaks glycogen down to glucose. Cellulose is present in the wood, and the fibre of clothes. Cell wall of bacteria is made up of cellulose.
solution_html:
Carbohydrate is essential for life and also used as storage molecules in plants and animals are as follows:

  1. The plant contains mainly starch, cellulose, sucrose, etc.
  2. Animal contains glycogen in their body. So, glycogen is also known as animal starch. Glycogen is present in the liver, muscles, and brain when the body needs glucose, the enzyme breaks glycogen down to glucose.
  3. Cellulose is present in the wood, and the fibre of clothes. Cell wall of bacteria is made up of cellulose.

question_text: Explain the terms primary and secondary structure of proteins. What is the difference between {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix and {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure of proteins?
question_html: Explain the terms primary and secondary structure of proteins. What is the difference between {tex}\alpha{/tex}-helix and {tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure of proteins?
solution_text: Primary structure: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide chain has a large number of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids linked to one another in a specific sequence. The specific sequence in which the various {tex}\alpha{/tex} -amino acids present in a protein are linked to one another is called its primary structure. Any change in this primary structure, i.e. a sequence of amino acids creates a different protein. Secondary structure : The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. Therefore, it gives the shape or conformation of the protein molecule This arises from regular folding of the backbone of a peptide bond with a hydrogen bond between the > C = 0 and N-H groups of different peptide bonds {tex}\alpha{/tex} -Helix structure: a-helix form is the most common form in which a polypeptide chain forms all possible types of hydrogen bonds by twisting into a right-handed screw (helix) with the -NH group of each amino acid residue hydrogen-bonded to -C = 0 group of the adjacent turn of the helix. The {tex}\alpha{/tex} -helix structure is also known as 3.613 helix. This represents that each turn of the helix contains approximately 3.6 amino acids and a 13 -member ring is formed by hydrogen bonding. The helix is held in its shape primarily by hydrogen bonds between one amide group and carbonyl group which is 3.6 amino acids units away {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure: In this conformation, the polypeptide chains lie side by side in a zigzag manner with alternate R groups on the same side situated at fixed distances apart. The two such neighboring polypeptide chains are held together by intermolecular H-bonds. A number of such chains can be interblended to form a sheet. These sheets are then stacked one above the other like the pages of this book to form a three-dimensional structure. This structure resembles pleated folds of drapery and hence is also called {tex}\beta{/tex} -pleated sheet structure.
solution_html:
Primary structure: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide chain has a large number of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids linked to one another in a specific sequence. The specific sequence in which the various {tex}\alpha{/tex}-amino acids present in a protein are linked to one another is called its primary structure. Any change in this primary structure, i.e. a sequence of amino acids creates a different protein.

Secondary structure: The secondary structure refers to the shape in which a long polypeptide chain can exist. Therefore, it gives the shape or conformation of the protein molecule This arises from regular folding of the backbone of a peptide bond with a hydrogen bond between the > C = 0 and N-H groups of different peptide bonds
{tex}\alpha{/tex}-Helix structure: a-helix form is the most common form in which a polypeptide chain forms all possible types of hydrogen bonds by twisting into a right-handed screw (helix) with the -NH group of each amino acid residue hydrogen-bonded to -C = 0 group of the adjacent turn of the helix. The {tex}\alpha{/tex}-helix structure is also known as 3.613 helix. This represents that each turn of the helix contains approximately 3.6 amino acids and a 13 -member ring is formed by hydrogen bonding. The helix is held in its shape primarily by hydrogen bonds between one amide group and carbonyl group which is 3.6 amino acids units away

{tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure: In this conformation, the polypeptide chains lie side by side in a zigzag manner with alternate R groups on the same side situated at fixed distances apart. The two such neighboring polypeptide chains are held together by intermolecular H-bonds. A number of such chains can be interblended to form a sheet. These sheets are then stacked one above the other like the pages of this book to form a three-dimensional structure. This structure resembles pleated folds of drapery and hence is also called {tex}\beta{/tex}-pleated sheet structure.

question_text: Write the structures of fragments produced on the complete hydrolysis of DNA. How are they linked in DNA molecule? Draw a diagram to show the pairing of nucleotide bases in the double helix of DNA.
question_html: Write the structures of fragments produced on the complete hydrolysis of DNA. How are they linked in DNA molecule? Draw a diagram to show the pairing of nucleotide bases in the double helix of DNA.
solution_text: Complete hydrolysis of the DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds called bases. In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is B-D-2-deoxyribose. Structures: Sugar: Phosphoric acid: Nitrogen base: DNA contains four bases viz, Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T). A unit formed by the attachment of a base to I'-position of sugar is called nucleoside. When nucleoside links to phosphoric acid at 5 -position of the sugar moiety, a nucleotide is formed. Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5 '-and 3 'carbon atoms of the pentose sugar. In DNA, two chains of the nucleic acid coil about each other and held together by H-bonds between bases of two chains.
solution_html:
Complete hydrolysis of the DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds called bases. In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is B-D-2-deoxyribose.
Structures:

  2. Phosphoric acid:
  3. Nitrogen base: DNA contains four bases viz, Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T).

Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5 '-and 3 'carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

    In DNA, two chains of the nucleic acid coil about each other and held together by H-bonds between bases of two chains.
